entry.title: What is planned for the 2605 release of SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing?
entry.content:
The 2605 release of SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing is planned to be deployed to BTP customer tenants on April 22, 2026:

Administration UI and Administration service:

Release

Test/Non-Productive & Productive Upgrades

2605

April 22, 2026

 

Variant Configuration service & Pricing service:

Release

Test/Non-productive Upgrade

Productive Upgrade

2605

April 22, 2026

May 13, 2026

 

This blog covers the following planned innovations:

  • Configuration and Pricing Services – Sunset of /closeSession
  • Configuration and Pricing Services – New Data Center: SAP Cloud Infrastructure in Europe (Rot) (cf-eu02)
  • Configuration and Pricing Services – End of Support for AWS Data Center in Europe (Frankfurt) (cf-eu11)
  • Pricing service – Extension Concept – Allow Creation/Updating of Pricing Attributes from Extensions
  • Pricing service – Extension Concept – Allow accessDateList as Input to Extensions
  • Pricing service – Support Access Type B
  • Pricing service – Extension Concept – Allow Overwriting Condition Rate Unit from Custom Extensions
  • Variant Configuration service – Optimized Read Access for Large Variant Tables with NonAtomic Cells
  • Variant Configuration service – Search by Quote Id

 

It also includes a call to act regarding the following changes:

  • Configuration and Pricing Services – Sunset of /closeSession

 

Configuration and Pricing Services – Sunset of /closeSession

The endpoints POST /closeSession of the configuration and pricing services used to be used to remove configurations and pricing documents from the HTTP session. This is no longer required because of improved resource management.

The endpoints were set to be deprecated with release 2505 and are now removed with the release 2605 as announced in the past.

Action: If called by your application, remove those calls before the release in May 2026.

More information was published with the 2025 May release in the API definition of the Pricing service and the Variant Configuration service and in the Development Guide.

 

Configuration and Pricing Services – New Data Center: SAP Cloud Infrastructure in Europe (Rot) (cf-eu02)

SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing is available in multiple hyperscaler regions. For EU Access contracts, configuration and pricing services will be available exclusively in SAP Cloud Infrastructure – Europe (Rot) (cf-eu02).

All configuration and pricing tenants of a customer must be operated in one data center (no mixing providers/regions). It is not possible to switch data centers for existing tenants.

 

Configuration and Pricing Services – End of Support for AWS Data Center in Europe (Frankfurt) (cf-eu11)

For EU Access contracts, SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing will no longer support AWS Europe (Frankfurt) EU Access (cf-eu11). For non-EU Access contracts, AWS Europe (Frankfurt) (cf-eu10) continues to be supported. EU Access contracts must use SAP Cloud Infrastructure Europe (Rot) (cf-eu02) going forward.

 

Pricing service – Extension Concept – Allow Creation/Updating of Pricing Attributes from Extensions

Requirement formulas can now create and update pricing attributes at runtime. This enables powerful scenarios where pricing context is enriched dynamically and immediately consumed by subsequent condition evaluations.

Key capabilities

  • Create new pricing attributes within requirement formulas.
  • Update existing attributes within requirement formulas.
  • Changes take effect immediately for all subsequent pricing condition evaluations.

Available scopes

  • Item‑Level Scope – Make attributes available across all pricing conditions for the entire item.
  • Condition‑Level Scope (default) – Restrict attributes to a specific pricing condition evaluation.

More information will be published with the May release in the Extension Guide (Pre-Production).

 

Pricing service – Extension Concept – Allow accessDateList as Input to Extensions

Pricing formulas can now access date information (for example, pricing and billing dates) from pricing items to implement time based logic without custom data retrieval.

How it works

  1. In action collect_attributes, declare the needed data. For example: accessDateList in the itemInput projection.
  2. At runtime, the service retrieves the relevant dates and passes them to action process_formula.

Use cases for custom routines:

  • Time dependent pricing logic
  • Contract validity and billing checks

More information will be published with the May release in the Extension Guide (Pre-Production).

 

Pricing service – Support Access Type B

During condition finding, it is possible to fetch values for fields marked with access type B directly from the found condition record and reuse them in subsequent accesses.

Out-of-the-box support – Sales Deals

  • For the standard Sales Deal field (KNUMA_AG), requirement formula 62 retrieves the Sales Deal from the current condition and exposes it for subsequent condition determination (no additional implementation required anymore).

Extensibility – custom attributes

  • For attributes beyond Sales Deal, implement custom requirement formulas. Together with the capability to create/update pricing attributes via extensions, you can enrich additional attributes and use them in access sequence logic.

More information will be published with the May release in the Extension Guide (Pre-Production).

 

Pricing service – Extension Concept – Allow Overwriting Condition Rate Unit from Custom Extensions

Pricing base and value formulas can now dynamically update the condition unit during execution — enabling flexible, unit-based pricing (for example commodity scenarios that convert PC → KGM at runtime). The formula evaluates the context and returns an updated condition unit for the condition evaluation.

More information will be published with the May release in the Extension Guide (Pre-Production).

 

Variant Configuration service – Optimized Read Access for Large Variant Tables with Non‑Atomic Cells

Access to large variant tables with non‑atomic cells required unnecessary time in specific scenarios. The read logic has been optimized, significantly improving response times when reading such tables through the /configurations APIs.

 

Variant Configuration service – Search by Quote Id

You can now filter stored configurations by a quote ID — for example:

GET /externalConfigurations?$filter=sourceId LIKE "*<quoteId>*"

You can query configurations where the sourceId contains the quote id at the beginning, end, or anywhere in the string. This makes it easy to retrieve all configurations related to a quote that contains several items (as soon as the caller provides the quote- and item id in the sourceId when creating a configuration).

More information will be published with the May release in the API Definition and in the Development Guide (Pre-Production).

 

With that, you have a good overview of what innovations are planned for the May release. Please check the updated roadmap and release notes after the release date to see which of these features were delivered as planned.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud UM: Utilizing of Parameter model in Writer function
entry.content:
Friendly Reminder

A basic understanding of APIs may be helpful to fully appreciate the context of this technical blog post.

Hi Everyone

SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM) offers powerful writing capabilities that let you transmit payloads to external applications via Connection, whether to SAP Analytics Cloud models, SOAP services, or OData services.

Now, I am here to share a short blog post highlighting how you can seamlessly configure a Parameterized model using Connections on write enabled APIs.

Before we proceed, let me first provide some context on parameterized models that leverage write enabled APIs.

When an OData Service Connection is created in SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model, it defines all available set of fields and parameters that can be used during modeling covering both fields for reading and the required parameters for writing.

These available sets of fields and parameters are being converted as a Model Entity from a successful Connection creation. To give you an example, let’s use this SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SAP SCT) API which is /GetQuantitativeMetricData.

For more information about this API, please see SAP SCT Analytical Use Case.

Using this GetQuantitativeMetricData POST request in SAP PaPM Cloud UM Connection will look like the image below. To have a better understanding, I have also added a brief description for each sections that are highlighted in the image.

  1. This was identified based from the SAP SCT Analytical Use Case which the POST request endpoint is GetQuantitativeMetricData .
  2. When you create the OData Service connection, SAP PaPM Cloud UM reads the $metadata and understands the fixed structure if the POST request requires Parameter Model.

    Therefore, the OData Service connection created will be defining the Parameter Model in its column.

  3. Once you have the details from the Parameter Model, you can identify what the Parameter Model is and then proceed to use this model in the connection by filling in the required fields.

Note: All of the Models from the image below are Sync Model and Connection as models from this endpoint GetQuantitativeMetricData are all required and contains relation.

After identifying all the required inputs and outputs, you can proceed with configuring the Writer function. Please note that the Writer function accepts a parameterized model only when the Model endpoint GetQuantitativeMetricData is used. Otherwise, the Writer function will detect the mismatch, and you will not be able to save the configuration. See image below:

But when you use the Parameter model as an input, since the Parameter Model is already defined from the creation of Connection, Writer function will not require you to perform a Field mapping.

If in any case you have accidentally mapped a field, then you will receive an information like the image below.

Once configured, you may now proceed on performing the Run of your Writer function with the calculated data records you have Written / Upload to Parameterized Model.

That concludes this blog post. I hope this helped demonstrate how SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model intelligently recognizes OData service structures, making configuration easier and more user‑friendly.

Thank you so much! Bye for now!

entry.title: Re: スーパー管理者の割当ついて
entry.content:
既存のスーパー管理者が自身の権限を他のユーザーにコピーすることで、
新規スーパー管理者を割り当てることが可能です。

 

そのため、任意の既存管理者へご連絡いただき、
スーパー管理者の割り当てをご依頼いただけますでしょうか。

詳細な操作方法につきましては、以下の KBA に記載しておりますので、ご参考になれば幸いです。

2596214 - How to maintain Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs listed in My Important Contacts - SAP for Me - SAP for Me 

---一部抜粋---

  • Add Super Administrator - An existing Super Administrator must provide all authorizations to another S-user ID.
    Tip: Follow KBA 1614304 to copy or clone their own authorizations.

上記内容について何か不明な点がございましたら、お気軽にご返信ください。

entry.title: Enhancing Your SAP Digital Manufacturing with Continuous Quality Checks
entry.content:
Implementing and operating SAP Digital Manufacturing is an important step in strengthening manufacturing processes, but it can also introduce challenges that may affect system stability and efficiency. To help address these concerns, we introduce the Continuous Quality Check (CQC) services for SAP Digital Manufacturing.
 
Continuous quality check (CQC) services are remote services embedded in your SAP Digital Manufacturing subscription and offered as part of your SAP Enterprise Support cloud edition maintenance contract at no extra cost.

Continuous Quality Checks for SAP Digital Manufacturing

Below is an overview of the CQC services available for SAP Digital Manufacturing.
 

Service

Benefit

CQC for Deployment Readiness 

Evaluates your SAP Digital Manufacturing tenant configuration and provides recommendations aligned with SAP best practices to support operational efficiency and a smoother transition to production. Evaluation includes SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Digital Manufacturing, and Project Management and Operational Support.

This service is ideal before go‑live for new implementation or additional rollout phases.

CQC for Solution Optimization

Performs a health check of your productive SAP Digital Manufacturing tenant. Evaluation includes SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Digital Manufacturing, and Project Management and Operational Support.

It identifies potential risks and improvement opportunities and provides SAP best‑practice recommendations as part of your post‑go‑live activities.

 

What's In It For You

  • A comprehensive view of your system configuration.
  • Safeguarding your upcoming go-live or ensuring your system health after go-live.
  • Avoiding common pitfalls that could impact performance and stability.


The Outcome

You will receive a service report that includes a detailed analysis of areas for improvement, a prioritized action plan and recommendations.

Why This Matters

Whether preparing for your first go‑live or optimizing your productive environment, CQC services deliver insights that help you proactively address potential issues, stabilize performance, and support continuous improvement.  Evaluation includes SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Digital Manufacturing, and Project Management and Operational Support.

For detailed guidance on how these continuous quality check services support system stability and risk mitigation, refer to our knowledge article, Optimizing SAP Digital Manufacturing Operations with Continuous Quality Checks.

Access the knowledge article today!


Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

Beyond continuous quality check, SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. Value maps on SAP Community bring together guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next - all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉  Watch our short demo video, then head over to our page to explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.  

 

About the author: 

Mabelle Roque Miguel is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Supply Chain Management solutions. Based in Singapore, Mabelle has been with SAP since 2007, taking on diverse roles that have largely centered around customer engagement and success. Now, her focus is on guiding businesses through their supply chain journey, helping them accelerate the adoption of SAP supply chain management solutions with value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

💡  Have questions or feedback on this content? Feel free to leave a comment below or submit them in the Q&A section.

entry.title: Re: Issue in Customer Clearing – Document Splitting Curre...
entry.content:
Good day @rohini_dutta21 

Thank you for your question.

The most likely reason behind the issue is document splitting inconsistency between the DA or DR documents. The clearing document is probably trying to derive split lines for currency type 10, but the base lines are zero or nearly zero, or the documents you are using carry different splitting characteristics such as profit center or segment. Because the clearing app does not allow those account assignment fields to be corrected interactively, the posting fails in the clearing step with GLT0004 style overflow behaviour.

Here are references for more guiding details.

3035677 - Sum of base lines equals zero in Document Splitting - SAP for Me

3588291 - After document splitting the amounts calculated for the general ledger view amount are much larger than expected - SAP for Me

3383754 - Account Assignment fields in Customer and Supplier line items in clearing applications - SAP for Me

Another configuration point is the fact that DR is being used as the journal entry type for clearing. Note that clearing app default type can be changed, so this should be reviewed and tested with the intended clearing type.

A possible was to resolve the issue you describe is to:

  • Use the Manage Journal Entry app to Compare the DA and DR items in the Entry View and the Ledger View

  • Check whether the split characteristics differ from each other
  • Then test the same scenario again in Clear Incoming Payments using the clearing/payment journal entry type you required instead of DR
  • If you align the source document assignments and you use the correct journal entry type, the clearing should post normally.
  • If the overflow still occurs, then raise an incident.

See the following references for more details.

3454558 - Change the default journal entry type - SAP for Me

3019716 - Different Amounts Shown in 'Verify General Journal Entries' and 'Manage Journal Entries' Apps for Same Journal Entry - SAP for Me

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/1c3273101f174e15acf9403bf88fcebc.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: he supplier down payment app is currently not visible...
entry.content:
Hello @paresh31837,

to your additional remark: "It works only if you are using MM-SD module, We want to post FI down payment only since we are not using MM-SD."

According to the App Library and the Process Navigator description it should be possible even if you only have accounting scope items J59 / J60 activated.

Unfortunately, I can not report from my actual knowledge, since we have FI as well as MM-SD scope items activated in our system.

However, these links show it should be possible with just FI scope items J59 / J60:

 

1) SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library F1688

 

2) Process Navigator J59 - SAP for Me: "also" implies both ways are possible, via J59 or via BJK (SD)

 

 

 

If you have those accounting scope items J59 / J60 activated and Down Payment is still not possible, I would suggest creating a support case with SAP directly at SAP for Me. Maybe they can give you a definite answer 🙂

 

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: How to get the internal UUID of a story calculated measure in SAC to be used in addMeasure(<UUID>) ?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am working in SAP Analytics Cloud with a chart based on a Datasphere live model.

I need to add a calculated measure (running totals) dynamically in an onSelect script. The scripting works when I use a UUID/GUID for some existing calculated measures that were created before by another developer.

Example:

Chart_1.addMeasure("92686182-6103-4765-8764-242384601111");

This works for some old calculated measures, so SAC is clearly accepting an internal UUID for those measures.

My problem is that for a new calculated measure I created, I cannot find the UUID.

What I tried:

  • Chart_1.getDataSource().getMeasures()
  • logging the full data source measures
  • trying the measure description
  • trying the user-defined measure ID

But getMeasures() only returns the backend/model measures from Datasphere, and my calculated measure is not included there.

So my questions are:

  1. In SAC scripting, how can I retrieve the internal UUID of a calculated measure created in the story?
  2. Is there any supported API to access story calculated measures for a chart based on a Datasphere live model?

Any guidance or best practice would be appreciated.

Thanks.

entry.title: IAM Release Activities: Restriction Category Appears Orange Despite No Visible Empty Restrictions
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to share an issue we encountered in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud during the IAM Release Activities for the 2602 upgrade. This information may help others facing similar inconsistencies in business role maintenance. 

Issue: 

During the post-upgrade role maintenance, we aimed to maintain all empty restrictions using the Maintain Business Roles app. 

However, we encountered cases like the example below where: 

  • The restriction category appeared in orange, indicating empty restriction fields. 
  • Opening the restriction category showed no visible empty fields. 
  • Under Restriction and Values, the system displayed "No data". 

 

Root Cause: 

The orange indicator is correct: the restriction category does contain empty restriction fields, even if they are not shown initially. 


Solution: 

Use the Add button to display and insert the missing restriction fields. After adding them, they can be maintained as required. 

 

Summary: 

Not all restrictions are displayed automatically. If the system indicates missing or empty restrictions, manually adding them may resolve the inconsistency.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud TRM – Approval process for T...
entry.content:
Good day @ktemma 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: Can SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud support an approval workflow after TRM deal settlement but before payment execution (F111), without using approval at bank master level so it won't impacting AP payments?

Answer: There is no dedicated Treasury-specific approval workflow at that exact point. The control point provided by the Public Cloud is payment approval at batch level, not an approval checkpoint inserted between deal settlement and F111.

 

Question 2: If payment-level approval is not supported, can approval be enforced before Fix / Post / Pay so that Treasury payments can only be created after Treasury Manager approval?

Answer: So, here again, the answer is not as a standard Treasury workflow gate. You can separate who fixes, posts, and pays, but that is not the same as a formal approval workflow before payment creation.

 

Question 3: If neither is possible, can approval be restricted to a dedicated Treasury payment method so that only TRM payments require approval and AP payments remain unaffected?

Answer: This is the closest standard design option I would recommend. However, there is an important qualification to note. Payment approval workflow behaviour can differ by payment method, because some payment methods are auto approved and can be changed only through SAP expert configuration. Which highlights payment method as a real discriminator in the BCM approval. In addition, payment batch approval happens in Approve Bank Payments, which is the standard approval point once the payment batch exists. So, based on that, the most realistic standard design is:

  • For you to use a dedicated Treasury payment method
  • Ensure TRM payment requests use that method
  • Let AP payments use different payment methods
  • Then apply payment approval only to the Treasury related payment batches.

Here are references for more guiding details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/e5ec5859d8e54df98492d80564a734c0/348edad7e55e4cd898a80ddcd3084e98.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/e5ec5859d8e54df98492d80564a734c0/4219c55368511d4be10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/59adb752bf730226e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

Therefore, the approach I would recommend is:

  1. Do not use bank account approval workflow as the main answer for your requirement, because that is what governs bank account master data lifecycle and can affect all payments that use that bank account.  See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/a11bed4241784388b9135705e0b1de5b.html?locale=en-US

  1. Rather, make use of payment batch approval as your standard approval point, because that is the SAP defined approval mechanism for outgoing payments.
  2. Make sure you isolate Treasury payments with a dedicated payment method and, if you need to, consider a dedicated payment run variant or payment identification. See below reference for further insights.

3632253 - Payment approval workflow not triggered for specific payment method - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

  1. Check your tenant whether Treasury F111 runs generate payment batches as expected. For TRM specific payment runs, some treasury runs may require Cross-Payment Run Payment Medium if the status is Payment not in Batch. See KBA 3385535 for more details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Creating Production Order Component Line Items via API
entry.content:
Hello,

Recently, we ran into the same problem, there is no API which enables you to create additional components in production orders.

We solved it with a locally developed API based on the RAP Business Object I_PRODUCTIONORDERTP  (entity I_ProductionOrderOperationTP has a create function for components) in Eclipse. In short we created a custom CDS to store our data, an ABAP class to process our data and a service to consume our data. After this you can use this custom API in your custom application the same way you would use a standard API.

Kind regards,

Rick

entry.title: SAP Financial Closing cockpit (no?) value added for users
entry.content:
Dear Community, 

I work in the global retail company with stores in over 30 countries. We have recently moved to using FCLOCO for Month End Closing tracking globally and general reception of the tool by the users is very bad. The tool is seen as over administration without any value added. The templates are not easy to customize. Tracking of task completion is manual even if the task itself is also done in SAP. There is no reporting board giving easy insight of the current status of MEC for the country or globally. Excel seems way more friendly than the cockpit. 

We are wondering if we are wrongly using FCLOCO or is this experience shared by other users. If you have good practices and practical hints, I will be very very grateful. 

Best regards, 

Tomasz

entry.title: Moving Average Price Not Updated and Zero Valuation at Goods Receipt in S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are currently facing an issue related to material costing and valuation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and we would appreciate your insights.

Context:

  • Scope Item 7NH (Universal Cost Component Breakdown) is activated.
  • We created a ticket with SAP to activate missing content related to scope items activated before release 2602. The synchronization has been completed, but no changes were observed in system behavior.
  • We have also recently created new valuation classes, which generated new G/L accounts.

Issue Description:

Case 1: Moving Average Price Not Updated
For materials that already have a valuation from the closing balance of December 31st (previous fiscal year), the system keeps the same value and does not update the moving average price after new transactions.

Case 2: Goods Receipt Not Valuated (0 Value)
For materials without any prior valuation (no opening balance), the system posts the Goods Receipt with a value of zero. As a result, the material is not valuated.

Additional Observation:
The manufacturing cost is correctly calculated and visible in the app "Analyze Production Costs – Event-Based", but it is not reflected in the material valuation or accounting postings.

Questions:

  • Could the creation of new valuation classes (and related G/L accounts) be the root cause of this issue?
  • Are there additional configuration steps required after creating new valuation classes in combination with scope item 7NH?
  • Is there any dependency with other scope items (e.g., inventory valuation or event-based production costing) that could explain this behavior?
  • Are we missing any integration step between CO (costing) and FI (material valuation)?

Any guidance or similar experiences would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support!

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: Performance and Goals - Story Reports
entry.content: Hello, what do you mean by "tracking changes made by each form participant..."? It is possible to report on different ratings (manager's, employee's ...) or comments. Actual change audits (previous value vs. new value) don't work. I'd recommend to start with the "All Templates" table for the performance forms, filter on your required template, then move on to "form" for form details (current step, doc ID, employee data) and then add additional tables as needed (eg. performance for overall form rating, competency for individual ratings, form section for custom field values ...)

entry.title: Re: What Is Agentic AI and Why SAP Teams Are Paying Attention in 2026
entry.content:
@venkatesanm 

Thank you for this well-structured and diplomatically worded response. Perhaps I am not understanding something vital here, but I still think it misses the core of what I was actually asking, and I want to press on that respectfully because I think the distinction matters for anyone making real deployment decisions.

You've described the standard agentic architecture clearly and I don't dispute the description. LLM as reasoning component, deterministic orchestration layer, constrained tool interfaces, observability and guardrails. That's the canonical pattern and it's accurately stated.

But my question wasn't "are LLMs deployed as unconstrained actors?" — I already assumed they are not. My question was about the implications of having a probabilistic, opaque reasoning component at the centre of the architecture at all, even when surrounded by controls, wrappers, guardrails, etc.

Let me try to articulate why I think your response, while accurate as a description of what is being built, doesn't resolve the concern.

The separation of cognition from control is real but not sufficient.

You write that "the LLM reasons; the system governs." That's a clean formulation, but it assumes that governance can be reliably applied to reasoning outputs without needing to understand how those outputs were produced. For many operational decisions this is probably fine. But the interesting cases, the ones that actually keep risk managers awake, are precisely the ones where the governance layer encounters an output that is within the permitted action space, passes schema validation and confidence thresholds, and is still wrong in a way that only becomes apparent downstream.

The guardrails catch what they were designed to catch. The residual risk lives in the gap between what the guardrails cover and what the LLM's opaque reasoning can produce. That gap may be small for routine decisions. But in mission-critical SAP workflows, "small but opaque" is exactly the risk profile that compliance frameworks are worst at handling.

Bounding where ambiguity is allowed to exist is the right goal but it concedes my original point.

You've framed the governance question as: "What decisions is the AI allowed to make autonomously, under what conditions, and with what auditability?"

I think that's the right question. But it implicitly concedes that the value of the LLM is confined to the space where we've decided that probabilistic, non-reproducible reasoning is acceptable. And that brings us back to the dilemma I outlined:

If the bounded decision space is narrow enough that guardrails can reliably contain the outputs, we should be asking whether a deterministic architecture could handle that space more cheaply, more transparently, and more auditably.

The LLM is fundamentally non-auditable, even to itself.

This point is not being explicitly acknowledged.

If the decision space is broad enough that the LLM's flexibility genuinely adds value over deterministic approaches, we should be honest that the guardrails are now operating over a larger and less predictable output space, and the residual risk increases accordingly.

The sweet spot I asked about in my previous comment — decisions too complex for rules engines but safe enough for opaque probabilistic reasoning — is real, but I still haven't seen it precisely characterised. And I think that characterisation is exactly what customers will need before they can make informed deployment decisions.

What I'd find most helpful here:

Rather than the general architectural pattern, which I think is well understood, I'd be interested to see:

  • Concrete examples of decisions within SAP workflows where the LLM reasoning layer demonstrably outperforms a deterministic alternative, with quantified improvement metrics.
  • A clear articulation of the residual risk profile in those cases — not "governance is achieved through architectural controls" in general terms, but what specific failure modes remain after the controls are applied, and what their estimated probability and impact are?
  • An honest assessment of the auditability gap — when an LLM-based agent makes a decision that passes all governance checks but later turns out to be wrong, what does the post-incident analysis look like? Can we actually trace the reasoning, or only the inputs and outputs? Because the architecture of the LLM itself says that we can't, and nor can it. It is quite likely to give a different answer, also for reasons it cannot articulate.

I think we're largely in agreement on the architecture and the intent. Where I'd push further is on whether the industry, and we as practitioners, have done the rigorous work of demonstrating that the LLM layer earns its place in mission-critical contexts, rather than assuming it does because the surrounding controls are well-designed.

This isn't scepticism about agentic AI as a concept. It's an insistence that the business case for the specific architectural choice of LLM-based reasoning be made explicitly, especially when we're advising customers who are trusting us (and it) with their mission-critical operations.

As a matter of strict semantics, the LLM does not reason as we understand that process, because it cannot. It follows a probabilistic path to a statistically likely answer. If you or our customers ask the AI that question, it will give them that answer.

Here is Claude 4.6 Opus' answer:

"The process is probabilistic. The parameters were learned through training on large text corpora. There is no separate reasoning module, no symbolic logic engine, no working memory that I inspect and report on. There is a forward pass through a neural network that produces a probability distribution over possible next tokens, and a sampling process that selects from that distribution.
I know what the process is, and it is not what people typically mean when they use the word reasoning. It is statistical pattern completion over learned representations. Whether you want to call that reasoning in some extended philosophical sense is a question for others to debate. But the operational characteristics are not ambiguous. The process is not transparent to inspection, not reproducible given identical inputs in all deployment conditions, and does not involve me evaluating the logical validity of my outputs before producing them."

entry.title: MRP Type VM – Reorder Point calculated much higher than expected when Period Indicator = Monthly
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts,

I am currently working on an inventory optimization project and encountered an issue with Automatic Reorder Point Planning (MRP Type VM) where the Reorder Point generated by SAP seems significantly higher than expected.

Below are the details of the configuration and data used.

Tank storage capacity: 800,000 kg

Historical Consumption (Last 6 Months)

Month Consumption (kg)

Aug1,892,613
Sep1,946,772
Oct1,915,658
Nov1,914,584
Dec1,771,639
Jan1,753,339

Average monthly demand ≈ 1,865,767 kg

Forecast Settings

Forecast Model: J (Automatic model selection)
Result after execution: D (Constant model)

Parameters:

  • Period Indicator: M (Monthly)
  • Historical periods: 6
  • Forecast periods: 6
  • Initialization: X
  • Model selection: A

MRP Settings

MRP Type: VM

MRP 1

  • Lot Size Procedure: EX

MRP 2

  • Planned Delivery Time: 5 days
  • GR Processing Time: 2 days
  • Total Lead Time: 7 days
  • Service Level: 90%

MRP 3

  • Period Indicator: M
  • Total Replenishment Lead Time: Blank

SAP Forecast Result

Safety Stock ≈ 52,000 kg

However, SAP calculates the Reorder Point ≈ 1,300,000 kg.

Manual Calculation (Expected Result)

Average monthly demand ≈ 1,865,767 kg

Daily demand:

1,865,767 / 30 ≈ 62,192 kg/day

Demand during lead time (7 days):

62,192 × 7 ≈ 435,344 kg

Expected ROP:

ROP ≈ 435,344 + 52,000 ≈ 487,000 kg

Issue Observed

The ROP generated by SAP (~1.3 million kg) is almost 3× higher than the expected value.

From my analysis, it appears that SAP might be using monthly demand directly instead of converting it into daily demand during lead time calculation.

Questions

  1. In MRP Type VM, does SAP calculate reorder point based on demand per forecast period (monthly) rather than converting it to daily demand when lead time is defined in days?
  2. If Period Indicator = Monthly, how does SAP internally convert demand to calculate demand during lead time?
  3. Is there any configuration or SAP Note that explains this behavior?
  4. Is this the standard behavior of SAP automatic reorder point planning, or could there be a missing parameter in the forecast or MRP settings?

Any clarification or insight would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: Performance and Goals - Story Reports
entry.content:
Hi martarodrigues8,

Good day!

There are some KB articles below that may help with the request:

  • 3385382 - Performance Forms Audit Trail Reporting - Performance Management - SAP for Me
  • 3520533 - How to get form Audit Trail details via Integration Center - SAP for Me

Please note an important limitation of the standard Story Report: it does not record the changes made by a participant in every step. If a participant updates a rating or comment multiple times, the report shows only the latest value (the most recent rating/comment), not the full edit history.

Meanwhile, the integration center is a more flexible (but more complex) way to fetch for example the rating changes in past steps. In the kba 3520533 it includes an example on how to get overall form rating by steps. You can use it as a reference to test if you could get the info needed from selecting different fields.

Best regards,
Makki

entry.title: New SAP Live Sessions for SAP administrators on SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
SAP will deliver two new waves of FREE SAP Live Sessions on SAP Analytics Cloud for administrators  in the second quarter of 2026 (Q2 2026) :

1/ SAP Live Sessions for administrators : Securing Content and Data in SAP Analytics Cloud
In this live session you will learn how to secure your content (stories, models, digital boardroom) and your application data in SAP Analytics Cloud. Upskill with our demos and learn our best practices on security with roles, sharing folder, data access control and model data privacy.

  • Securing Content and Data in SAP Analytics Cloud : Apr 28, 2026, 09:30 - 11:00 Europe/Paris
  • Securing Content and Data in SAP Analytics Cloud : Jun 16, 2026, 16:00 - 17:30 Europe/Paris

In these SAP Live sessions, an instructor will be available to answer your questions live ! Watch the recording if you are not available on these dates.

Register here (free) : https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions/securing-content-and-data-in-sap-analytics-cloud

2/ SAP Live Sessions for administrators : Exploring Data Connections for On-Premise Datasources in SAP Analytics Cloud
This live session will cover steps and best practices for creating connections in SAP Analytics Cloud. In the first part, steps and best practices will be shown to create import data connections from a SQL Database and a File Server with the SAP Cloud Connector and the SAP Analytics Cloud agent. In a second part, steps and best practices will be shown to create live data connections to SAP HANA, SAP S/4HANA and SAP Universe. A lot of demonstrations will be used throughout to illustrate concepts.

  • Exploring Data Connections for On-Premise Datasources in SAP Analytics Cloud : Apr 29, 2026, 09:30 - 11:00 Europe/Paris
  • Exploring Data Connections for On-Premise Datasources in SAP Analytics Cloud : Jun 17, 2026, 16:00 - 17:30 Europe/Paris

In these SAP Live sessions, an instructor will be available to answer your questions live ! Watch the recording if you are not available on these dates.

Register here (free) : https://learning.sap.com/live-sessions/exploring-data-connections-for-on-premise-datasources-in-sap-analytics-cloud

We are looking for seeing you at these SAP Live sessions.

Regards,
Pierre LANSALOT-BASOU
Instructor, SAP Learning France

entry.title: Re: How to create background job for PDS generation?
entry.content:
You don’t need **/SAPAPO/CURTO_CREATE** anymore because that classic report isn’t available in S/4HANA. For automated PDS generation, use **/SAPAPO/CURTO_ADV_CREATE** instead and create a **variant** for it in transaction **SE38/SA38**, then schedule it as a background job in **SM36** on an hourly or daily cycle. This is the standard and supported approach in APO‑PP/DS on S/4HANA.

Best regards,
Tamara
concoracard com

entry.title: Re: Event Mesh + AMQP: The Retry Trap Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
@MateuszPiotrowski   Thanks for the hint, I am already exploring this DataStore extension solution.   It is basically tries to mimic what SAP Process Integration has built-in.
In SAP PI, when the message fails it will be saved in the database for a later use, and when a user clicks on the 'resend message' button, the restart process will read the message from the database and will place it back to the inbound queue from which the message will be reprocessed.

Sorry , but I still cannot comprehend how a production environment with hundreds of interfaces can be supported without this individual message reprocessing function, which SAP PI has by default.

If you have production environment experiences can you please share how the error handling is supported with only built in available solution like Event Mesh or JMS?
I am just asking because you told that Event Mesh solution is your preferred option for message reprocessing perspective.  But it turned out that this option cannot handle individual reprocessing, even with the use of the pipeline DataStore extension because it is tailored for JMS.

It seems for me that a new blogpost should be created with a title something like:
"SAP IS is lacking of a major functionality that Nobody Warns You About" 

I am definitely missing out something here. Your inputs would be very valuable.
Thank you!

entry.title: Building a CSV Bulk Import Feature in SAP CAP for Incident Management
entry.content:
Introduction :

Data infrequently enters systems one record at a time in business operations. The most functional armies  cipher using spreadsheets,  heritage tools, or bulk uploads from external systems.

 Consider an operations  team requesting that hundreds of incidents be entered into an incident  operation system. Manually creating these records via the  stoner interface is  delicate and time- consuming.

 We  enforced a CSV- grounded bulk import  point in an Incident Management operation  erected with SAP CAP( Node.js) to  break this issue.

 This blog describes how to use database deals, Node.js, and CAP services to  produce and  apply a scalable CSV upload channel.

 By the time this companion ends, you will know how to CSV lines can be uploaded to a CAP service.

* Use Node.js to parse CSV data

* corroborate and  transfigure data

 * effectively fit several records into the database

* Use the applicable bulk import patterns for business  operations.

 

Real-World Use Case

 Enterprise results  frequently produce bulk import capabilities. a common scripts  correspond of  following features

* Bringing in incidents from current systems

* Integrating  customer information from  heritage platforms

* advertisement fresh incidents that have been reported on  colorful platforms

* Users can upload a CSV train with structured data to resettle both new and being incident records  rather of manually creating records.

 

Solution Architecture / Workflow :

The armature of the CSV import  point is straightforward but scalable.

 Step 1: The  stoner uploads the structured data- containing Excel  train

 Step 2: Action for CAP Services

 Step 3: Subcaste of CSV Parsing

 Step 4: confirmation of Data

 Step 5: Insert into the database

 Step 6: Error/ Success response

 

Crucial factors of the result:

Allow  users to upload CSV lines for the purpose of UI Upload. CAP Service--> Get the train and initiate the import sense Node.js Parser--> Transforms CSV rows into JSON Objects Validation Layer--> Ensures data quality Database Insert--> Stores records in SAP HANA


Step 1 — Define the Incident Entity

First we define the Incident entity inside schema.cds.

namespace incident;

entity Incidents {
  key ID        : UUID;
  title         : String(255);
  description   : String(500);
  priority      : String(20);
  status        : String(20);
  createdAt     : Timestamp;
}

 

Step 2 — Define the CSV Import Action

Next we define a custom CAP action responsible for processing the uploaded file.

using { sap.capire.incidents as db } from '../db/schema.cds';

service ProcessorService {
  .draft.enabled
  entity Incidents as projection on db.Incidents;

   entity Customers as projection on db.Customers;
   entity Status    as projection on db.Status;
   entity Urgency   as projection on db.Urgency;
}

service ImportService @(path: 'import') {
  action importCSV(csv : LargeString) returns String;
}

Step 3 — Install CSV Parsing Library

To process CSV data, install a parsing library.

npm i csv-parse

Step 4 — Implement the Import Logic

Inside service.js, we implement the backend logic.

const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const { parse } = require('csv-parse/sync');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {

  const { Incidents } = this.entities;

  this.on('importIncidents', async (req) => {

    try {

      const csvData = req.data.file.toString();

      const records = parse(csvData, {
        columns: true,
        skip_empty_lines: true
      });

      const incidents = records.map(row => ({
        title: row.title,
        description: row.description,
        priority: row.priority,
        status: row.status,
        createdAt: new Date()
      }));

      await INSERT.into(Incidents).entries(incidents);

      return `${incidents.length} incidents imported successfully`;

    } catch (error) {

      req.error(500, "CSV Import Failed");

    }

  });

});

Step 5 — Implement an action handler

A button is created after which file explorer will open and choose of csv file can be made . 

sap.ui.define([
  "sap/ui/core/mvc/ControllerExtension",
  "sap/m/MessageToast",
  "sap/m/MessageBox"
], function (ControllerExtension, MessageToast, MessageBox) {
  "use strict";

  return ControllerExtension.extend("ns.incidents.ext.controller.Import", {
    override: {
      onInit: function () {}
    },

    onOpenImportDialog: function () {
      const input = document.createElement("input");
      input.type = "file";
      input.accept = ".csv,text/csv";

      input.onchange = async (e) => {
        const file = e.target.files && e.target.files[0];
        if (!file) return;

        try {
          const csvText = await file.text();
          await this._callImportAction(csvText);
        } catch (err) {
          MessageBox.error(err?.message || String(err));
        }
      };

      input.click();
    },

    _callImportAction: async function (csvText) {
      try {
        const oView = this.base.getView();

        // ✅ Must exist if manifest model "import" is configured correctly
        const oImportModel = oView.getModel("import");
        if (!oImportModel) {
          MessageBox.error(
            'Import model not found. Check manifest.json: models -> "import" and dataSources -> "importService".'
          );
          return;
        }

        const oOp = oImportModel.bindContext("/importCSV(...)", null, { $$groupId: "$direct" });
        oOp.setParameter("csv", csvText);

        const oResultContext = await oOp.execute();
        const resultObj = oResultContext?.getObject?.();

        // V4 primitive often returns { value: "..." }
        const msg = (resultObj && typeof resultObj === "object" && "value" in resultObj)
          ? resultObj.value
          : resultObj;

        MessageToast.show(msg || "Import finished.");
      } catch (e) {
        MessageBox.error(e?.message || "Import failed.");
      }
    }
  });
});

Step 6 — Upload the csv file which you want to upload 

When the CSV file is uploaded, the CAP action processes the file and inserts records.

 

Step 7 — Upload and TestEverything is  set and ready to go.

 This is how it operates

  1. Launch the Report on the Incidents List.
  2. Elect Import.
  3. Elect the CSV  train.

 After the  operation retrieves the  train, CAP parses it and deposits the records into SAP HANA.

 

 

Conclusion:

structure a complete CSV import  point for a CAP Fiori Elements  operation was covered in this blog.

 Along the route, you observed • How to make an import CAP action,

  • Using Node.js to parse CSVs
  • Entering data into SAP HANA
  • Managing  train uploads directly within the cybersurfer and calling CAP  conduct from the Fiori Elements  stoner interface.

Expanding this pattern is fairly easy; consider batch processing, Excel  significances, data  confirmation, import trials, error reports, and so forth.

Adding CSV  significances is simple with CAP, which fits in nicely with a clean, enterprise armature. It's a practical answer to a typical business  demand.

entry.title: What is the easiest way to extract SAP Concur and Fieldglass data for contingent workforce spend ana
entry.content:
Procurement manager trying to get a consolidated view of contingent workforce spend. We use fieldglass for contingent labor management and concur for expense reporting but the data lives in two completely separate systems with different data models and different reporting capabilities. Finance wants a single view that shows total contingent workforce cost including both the staffing agency fees in fieldglass and the travel and expense reimbursements in concur for those same workers.

The embedded reporting within each system covers basic operational stuff but it's not sufficient for the kind of cross source analysis finance actually needs. You can't blend fieldglass staffing data with concur expenses with ariba procurement in sap's native reporting, and self service analytics is basically nonexistent within the apps themselves. Joining them externally requires matching worker records across systems that use different identifiers. Fieldglass uses sow ids and worker ids while concur uses employee ids and cost center codes. Some contingent workers are in concur as temporary employees, others file expenses through the agency which shows up differently.

I've looked at using sap analytics cloud to consolidate but getting the data into sac from both systems requires setting up connections that our basis team doesn't have capacity for. We also need this data in our main analytics warehouse alongside other procurement data from ariba for a complete category spend view. Is anyone successfully combining fieldglass and concur data for workforce analytics?

entry.title: Re: NO Foreign trade reporting in the public cloud (like...
entry.content:
Good day @Angela2 

Thank you for your question.

You are probably not missing a hidden classic foreign trade reporting feature. The Public Cloud approaches this differently: It provides country or region-specific Balance of Payments/Central Bank Payment statutory reports in Run Statutory Reports, and you should, where possible, automatically derive reporting attributes such as LZB or SCB indicator, not enforce only through manual entry plus validation.

So, what I recommend is:

  1. Verify whether your requirement report is already available in Run Statutory Report
  2. Implement derivation logic for the LZB or SCB indicator and supplying country based on the guidance mentioned in KBA 2845258
  3. Keep the validation rule only as your fallback control option
  4. Avoid spending time looking for an SCB indicator maintenance in SSCUI in Public Cloud, because this maintenance option is not available.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6833660c7722498fb41584b098420f89/556604558d6447779076737a0de21764.html?locale=en-US

3486912 - Unable to create/maintain SCB Indicator - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: File Not Received in SAP PO After Server Change – Sender Channel Conflict Issue
entry.content:
Scenario Overview:

In our integration landscape, the data flow is:

AccordFT (MFT) → FTP → SAP PO → File (Archival) → SAP PO → SAP CAR/POSDTA

We are using a single standard interface and mapping for all countries. File processing is dynamic, and files are handled based on the country code.The MFT team confirmed that SAF files were successfully processed and sent from their end. However, SAP PO did not receive the files for one specific country, and the data was not delivered to the SAP CAR system.

Issue:

  • Files were successfully sent from MFT
  • No messages were visible in SAP PO for one country
  • Other country files were processed successfully using the same interface
  • Data was not reaching SAP CAR

Additionally, the issue was difficult to trace because:

  • No explicit errors were observed
  • Configuration appeared correct and consistent
    Cause
  • The issue occurred after replacing the old PO server with a new server in the ICO configuration.

The scenario was successfully tested in QA without any issues, as the system was still pointing to the old server. This created a false positive validation, and the issue only surfaced in Production after switching to the new server.
QA:

In Production:

  • The configuration was updated to use the new server
  • The issue appeared only after this change

Further analysis revealed that:

  • There were only two ICOs and two sender channels with similar configurations
    These existed because:
  • One set was originally used for the old server
  • Another set was created for the new server configuration
  • After replacing the old server with the new one, both configurations became effectively identical, leading to overlap

Additionally, the interface design included connected flows:

  • One flow handles file pickup and archival in SAP PO
  • Another flow processes and delivers data to SAP CAR

Due to similar sender channel configurations (same server and path, different users), one channel interfered with file pickup. Because of the connected interface design:

  • The file was not picked correctly in the inbound flow
  • As a result, downstream processing to SAP CAR did not occur
  • Since the configurations were nearly identical and no errors were thrown, the issue was not immediately visible.

Solution:

To resolve the issue:

  • Reviewed all sender channels and ICOs for overlapping configurations
  • Identified the duplicate/conflicting sender channel
  • Stopped the unnecessary sender channel
  • Requested the MFT team to resend the SAF file

After implementing the fix:

  • SAP PO successfully picked up the file
  • The archival flow executed correctly
  • The file was processed and delivered to SAP CAR

    Key Takeaways:
  • Avoid maintaining duplicate ICOs and sender channels with identical configurations
  • Ensure clear separation when transitioning from old to new servers
  • Validate end-to-end flows, especially when interfaces are interconnected (archival + processing flows)
  • Always test using the actual target server (Production-like setup)
  • Similar configurations without errors can still cause silent processing conflicts

entry.title: Re: AI driven Composite Creation in SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Hi @zee_siddique15,

Once you add the custom widget to the story, make sure to use the same dataset that was originally uploaded for the widget.

Also, ensure that the measures and dimensions are added in the exact order specified in the JSON file (under the MyDataBinding section). This ordering is important for the widget to interpret the data correctly.

The widget should automatically infer the timeline (including where the forecast begins) based on the data itself, so you typically won’t need to manually provide a “Forecast Start Date” within SAC.

entry.title: Auto-Populating the External Actual Transfer of Control Date on POD Confirmation
entry.content:
Introduction

In the 5MQ (Intercompany Sales – Delivery-Based) scenario in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the External Transfer of Control Date on the outbound delivery plays a critical role in intercompany billing accuracy. This date signals the moment when legal ownership of goods transfers to the external party — a key input for revenue recognition and intercompany invoice generation.

By default, the Actual Date under Header Details → Shipment → External Transfer of Control Dates must be entered manually by the warehouse or logistics team. This introduces the risk of delays, inconsistencies, or simply being forgotten.

This blog post describes a clean BAdI-based enhancement that automatically sets the Actual Date to the current system date the moment a warehouse operator confirms the Proof of Delivery (POD) in the Outbound Delivery change transaction.

Business Requirement

Scenario: 5MQ – Intercompany Sales with Outbound Delivery

Trigger: The user opens an outbound delivery, navigates to the Overview POD tab, checks "Confirm Proof of Delivery", and clicks Save.

Desired behavior: At the moment of Save, the field:

Header Details → Shipment tab → External Transfer of Control Dates → Actual Date

…should be automatically populated with today's system date.

This eliminates manual entry, ensures audit-trail consistency, and aligns the transfer of control date with the POD confirmation event.

Technical Approach: BAdI LE_SHP_MODIFY_HEAD

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides the extensibility BAdI LE_SHP_MODIFY_HEAD (Modify Outbound Delivery Header) through the Custom Logic app (transaction: Manage Your Solution → Adapt Logic). This BAdI fires during delivery processing whenever the delivery header data is modified — including during POD confirmation.

Interface parameters of interest:

Parameter Direction Description

delivery_document_inImportCurrent state of the delivery header
delivery_document_outExportModified delivery header to be applied
messagesExportMessages to display to the user

Key field:

 
INTCOEXTACTLTRANSFOFCTRLDTETME
Description : External Actual Transfer of Control Date
Type        : Packed number, Length 8 (TIMESTAMP format: YYYYMMDDHHMMSS)


Implementation

Step 1 – Navigate to Custom Logic

Go to: Manage Your Solution → Adapt Logic → search for BAdI LE_SHP_MODIFY_HEAD → Create Implementation.

Step 2 – Write the Enhancement Code

Paste the following code into the Custom Logic editor and click Save and Publish:

 
" Always copy input to output first (mandatory pattern)
delivery_document_out = delivery_document_in.

" Only trigger when POD is confirmed (status = 'C')
IF delivery_document_in-overallproofofdeliverystatus = 'C'.

  " Build YYYYMMDDHHMMSS timestamp from current system date
  " Note: cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date() returns an ordinal
  " in ABAP Cloud – we must use ISO string formatting to get YYYYMMDD
  DATA(lv_date_str) = |{ cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date( ) DATE = ISO }|.
  REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '-' IN lv_date_str WITH ''.
  " Result: '20260331'

  DATA lv_ts TYPE timestamp.
  lv_ts = |{ lv_date_str }000000|.
  " Result: 20260331000000 (midnight UTC of the current date)

  " Write the timestamp to the External Actual Transfer of Control Date
  delivery_document_out-intcoextactltransfofctrldtetme = lv_ts.

ENDIF.

Step 3 – Test

  1. Open an outbound delivery in the POD change transaction.
  2. Check "Confirm Proof of Delivery" → POD Status changes to C (Confirmed).
  3. Click Save.
  4. Navigate to Header Details → Shipment tab → External Transfer of Control Dates.
  5. Verify that Actual Date is now populated with today's date.

 

Key Technical Learnings

This implementation required resolving several non-obvious challenges:

1. Always assign delivery_document_out = delivery_document_in first

The BAdI output parameter starts empty. Failing to copy the input first will wipe all delivery header data. This is the mandatory first line of any LE_SHP_MODIFY_HEAD implementation.

2. Check overallproofofdeliverystatus, not mode

The BAdI does not have a dedicated "POD confirmation" mode flag. The correct way to detect the confirmation event is to check:

 
delivery_document_in-overallproofofdeliverystatus = 'C'

The BAdI fires on every user interaction (including simply opening the delivery in change mode), so this condition scopes the logic to the correct moment.

3. cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date() returns an ordinal number

In ABAP Cloud, TYPE d stores dates as ordinal day counters (e.g., 739707 for March 31, 2026), not as the classic YYYYMMDD character string. Directly assigning this to a packed-number timestamp field stores an incorrect value that the UI cannot interpret — resulting in a blank display.

The correct pattern is to use the ISO date template literal:

 
DATA(lv_date_str) = |{ cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date( ) DATE = ISO }|.
" Produces: '2026-03-31' (semantic string, not ordinal)

This leverages ABAP's string formatting to produce the human-readable ISO date regardless of internal representation.

4. The field INTCOEXTACTLTRANSFOFCTRLDTETME is a TIMESTAMP (P LENGTH 8), not a DATE

Despite appearing as a "date" field in the UI, the underlying type is a 14-digit packed timestamp (YYYYMMDDHHMMSS). Writing only the 8-digit date portion (20260331) results in an invalid date — the system interprets it as year=0002, month=02, day=60, which renders as blank.

The correct value must include a time component:

 
lv_ts = |{ lv_date_str }000000|.  " → 20260331000000

Note on timezone: Writing 000000 (midnight UTC) will display as 08:00 in a UTC+8 system — this is expected timezone conversion behavior and is perfectly acceptable for date-stamping purposes.

Summary

Aspect Detail
BAdILE_SHP_MODIFY_HEAD
Trigger conditionoverallproofofdeliverystatus = 'C'
Target fieldINTCOEXTACTLTRANSFOFCTRLDTETME
Field typeTYPE timestamp (P LENGTH 8, YYYYMMDDHHMMSS format)
Date retrievalcl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date( ) with DATE = ISO formatting
Value written20260331000000 (date + 000000 for time)

This implementation is fully cloud-compliant (no restricted APIs, no direct DB access), requires no transport, and is immediately active after publishing through the Custom Logic app.

Tested on: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 2602 release

entry.title: Research Survey: Building an AI Agent for Intelligent Order Combination - Your SAP Expertise Needed!
entry.content:
Order management is one of the most critical yet complex processes in any SAP environment. Whether you're dealing with purchase orders, sales orders or production orders, the challenge of efficiently combining and optimizing these orders while maintaining accuracy and compliance is something every SAP professional faces daily. We're in the early stages of exploring how AI could transform this process and we need your expertise to guide us in the right direction.

What We're Building

The Challenge

Currently, order combination processes often involve:

  • Manual review and decision-making that's time-consuming and error-prone
  • Complex business rules that vary significantly between organizations
  • Integration challenges across different SAP modules and systems
  • Difficulty balancing efficiency gains with accuracy requirements
  • Limited visibility into optimization opportunities
Our Vision

We're exploring the development of an AI agent that could assist with intelligent order combination by:

  • Understanding your specific business rules and constraints
  • Learning from historical order patterns and outcomes
  • Suggesting optimal combinations based on multiple criteria
  • Integrating seamlessly with your existing SAP processes
  • Providing transparent reasoning for its recommendations

The goal isn't to replace human judgment, but to augment it with intelligent insights that help you make better, faster decisions.

Why We Need YOU

Your Experience Matters

We're seeking input from SAP professionals who work with order processes daily:

  • Procurement specialists and buyers
  • Sales order management teams
  • Production planners and schedulers
  • SAP functional consultants
  • Business analysts working with order-to-cash or procure-to-pay processes
  • Anyone involved in order optimization and combination decisions
What We Want to Learn

Your real-world experience is invaluable for understanding:

  • Current pain points and manual workarounds in order combination
  • Business rules and criteria that drive combination decisions
  • Integration requirements with existing SAP systems
  • User interface preferences and workflow needs
  • Success metrics and quality measures that matter most
  • Potential concerns or barriers to AI adoption in this area

About This Survey

Participation Details
  • Time Required: 8-12 minutes
  • Confidentiality: All responses are completely anonymous
  • Who Should Participate: SAP professionals involved in any aspect of order management, combination or optimization
What We're Asking About
  • Your current order combination processes and challenges
  • Decision criteria and business rules you use today
  • Desired features and capabilities for an AI assistant
  • Implementation preferences and success factors

Take the Survey

Survey Link - Share Your Experience Here

Your insights will directly shape how we approach this requirements engineering effort and ensure any future solution addresses real business needs rather than theoretical problems.

Join the Conversation

Have additional thoughts about AI in order management? Share them in the comments below! We'd love to hear about specific use cases, success stories or concerns you might have. Please also share this survey with colleagues who work with order processes - the more diverse perspectives we gather, the better we can understand the full scope of requirements.

Thank You

Thank you for taking the time to share your expertise with us. Requirements engineering is most successful when it's grounded in real user needs and experiences and your participation makes that possible.

For questions about this research or to discuss order management challenges in your environment, feel free to reach out through the comments or SAP Community messaging.

 

 

 

Tags

#artificial-intelligence #order-management #requirements-engineering #business-process-optimization #user-research #sap-integration

entry.title: Re: questions about x-csrf-token for SAP OData APIs
entry.content: Hi,
